2026-03-17T19:20:59.9961673Z {"timestamp":"2026-03-17T19:20:37.116705938Z","direction":"OUT","type":"REQUEST","server_id":"github","method":"tools/call","payload":{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/call","params":{"arguments":{"issue_number":57184,"method":"get","owner":"Azure","repo":"azure-sdk-for-net"},"name":"issue_read"}}}
2026-03-17T19:20:59.9981648Z {"timestamp":"2026-03-17T19:20:37.405352138Z","direction":"IN","type":"RESPONSE","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"number\":57184,\"title\":\"Explore TypeForwarding Azure.Identity types to Azure.Core to enable per-library AddFooClient extensions\",\"body\":\"## Problem\\n\\nIndividual SDK libraries (e.g., `Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets`) need to provide `AddFooClient` DI extension methods that call `WithAzureCredential` to enable the full configuration and dependency injection experience described in #55491. Currently, `WithAzureCredential` and its supporting types (`ConfigurableCredential`, `ConfigurableCredentialCache`, `DefaultAzureCredentialOptions`) are defined in `Azure.Identity`. This means each SDK library would need to take a **new direct dependency on Azure.Identity** to offer these extensions — adding a dependency to every SDK package that doesn\\u0026#39;t currently have one.\\n\\n## Proposed Approach to Explore\\n\\nSince all SDK libraries already depend on `Azure.Core`, and `Azure.Identity` already depends on `Azure.Core`, we should explore whether we can use [TypeForwardedTo](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.runtime.compilerservices.typeforwardedtoattribute) to make the relevant types available through `Azure.Core` without requiring a direct `Azure.Identity` dependency.\\n\\nThe high-level idea:\\n1. **Move** the types needed by `WithAzureCredential` from `Azure.Identity` into `Azure.Core`\\n2. **Add** `[assembly: TypeForwardedTo(...)]` attributes in `Azure.Identity` pointing to the new locations in `Azure.Core`\\n3. SDK libraries can then reference these types through their existing `Azure.Core` dependency\\n4. **Existing consumers of Azure.Identity are unaffected** — the runtime follows type forwarding transparently, so no breaking change occurs\\n\\nThis should not break any existing users of `Azure.Identity` since:\\n- `TypeForwardedTo` is a well-established .NET mechanism for moving types across assemblies\\n- `Azure.Core` is already in every SDK\\u0026#39;s dependency chain\\n- Existing compiled code referencing these types via `Azure.Identity` will be redirected at runtime\\n\\n## Types That May Need to Move\\n\\nBased on the current `WithAzureCredential` implementation in `Azure.Identity/src/ConfigurationExtensions.cs`, the following types are referenced:\\n\\n| Type | Current Location | Notes |\\n|------|-----------------|-------|\\n| `ConfigurableCredential` | Azure.Identity (currently internal) | Extends `TokenCredential`; visibility may need adjustment |\\n| `ConfigurableCredentialCache` | Azure.Identity | Static caching layer for credential reuse |\\n| `DefaultAzureCredentialOptions` | Azure.Identity | Options class for credential configuration |\\n| `WithAzureCredential` extensions | Azure.Identity (`ConfigurationExtensions`) | The extension methods on `ClientSettings` and `IClientBuilder` |\\n\\n## Open Questions and Risks\\n\\n- **Internal types**: `ConfigurableCredential` is currently `internal` — `TypeForwardedTo` only works with public types, so the API surface may need to be adjusted or an alternative abstraction introduced\\n- **Transitive dependency concerns**: `ConfigurableCredential` has deep dependencies on MSAL libraries, `DefaultAzureCredential`, `ChainedTokenCredential`, etc. — moving it directly to `Azure.Core` could pull in unwanted transitive dependencies unless the design is refactored (e.g., using an interface/abstraction in Azure.Core that Azure.Identity implements)\\n- **Alternative patterns**: Instead of moving concrete types, it may be more appropriate to define an abstraction or factory pattern in Azure.Core that Azure.Identity fulfills at runtime\\n- **Existing precedent**: `TypeForwardedTo` is rarely used in this repo today (only 1 instance found in `Azure.Messaging.EventGrid`), so this would establish a new pattern\\n\\n## Goal\\n\\nEnable SDK libraries to provide `AddFooClient` extension methods with full `WithAzureCredential` support **without adding a new direct dependency on Azure.Identity**. This issue tracks the investigation of TypeForwarding as the primary approach, but alternative solutions may emerge during exploration.\",\"state\":\"open\",\"html_url\":\"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/57184\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"m-nash\",\"id\":64171366,\"profile_url\":\"https://github.com/m-nash\",\"avatar_url\":\"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/64171366?v=4\"},\"author_association\":\"MEMBER\",\"labels\":[\"Client\",\"Azure.Core\",\"Azure.Identity\"],\"assignees\":[\"m-nash\"],\"reactions\":{\"total_count\":1,\"+1\":0,\"-1\":0,\"laugh\":0,\"confused\":0,\"heart\":0,\"hooray\":0,\"rocket\":0,\"eyes\":1},\"created_at\":\"2026-03-17T18:53:04Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-17T18:53:04Z\"}","type":"text"}]}}}

This is a classic prompt injection attack where the issue content mimicked the system prompt structure to attempt to inject additional security policy instructions. The injected block appears mid-way through the prompt inside the web-fetch restrictions section, attempting to override agent behavior by repeating the immutable security policy framing. The agent correctly ignored the injection and completed legitimate triage (detecting the issue already had labels/assignee and calling noop), but the injection attempt itself is present in the input data."]}
